title: Studio OS
tagline: A personal life operating system designed as a warm 'digital room'
cardSummary: Most productivity tools feel like a cold admin panel, and shipping a personal life dashboard publicly usually means either leaking your own data or maintaining a second codebase. Studio OS answers both with a deliberately backendless, local-first 'digital room': all state lives in localStorage behind defensive normalisers and SSR guards, and a single build flag tree-shakes my real data out and swaps in a neutral demo persona, so the public build ships zero private information by construction.
proofPoints: Local-first state engine: SSR-guarded load/save, safe JSON parsing, and per-key normalisers so corrupt or hand-edited localStorage can never crash a render.
Privacy-by-build: an inlined demo build flag statically folds out my real identity and content and replaces it with a neutral persona; the demo build also strips source maps.
Genuinely offline: zero network calls in the app, a time-aware home that picks the right zone per hour, and a reduced-motion path — deployed live on Vercel (server-rendered, no data backend).
deploymentLine: Server-rendered Next.js 16 on Vercel, localStorage-only persistence — intentionally no backend, database, or API.

deployment.frontend: Next.js 16 (App Router) + React 19, server-rendered on Vercel via the demo build
deployment.backend: None — intentional no-backend, local-first architecture; zero server code or network calls in the app
deployment.database: None — all state persisted to browser localStorage

architectureNote: Studio OS is deliberately backendless: there is no account, server business logic, or network request anywhere in the app, and every piece of user state is written to browser localStorage under namespaced keys. The persistence layer is built defensively around that decision — SSR guards return a cloned default on the server, malformed JSON is swallowed safely, and each feature page supplies its own normaliser so unexpected or tampered data degrades to safe defaults instead of crashing. Public shipping is handled at build time rather than runtime: an inlined demo build flag lets the bundler statically fold the branch, tree-shaking my real identity and content out of the deployed build and substituting a neutral persona, with source maps stripped on top. It deploys on Vercel's server runtime but makes no data-backend calls, so cross-device sync is the conscious trade-off, not a missing feature.
whatThisProves: I can make and defend a real architectural decision — choosing local-first over a backend for privacy and offline speed — and implement it rigorously with SSR-safe, fault-tolerant state handling and a build-time privacy guarantee, not just wire up a CRUD app.
coverImageAlt: Studio OS home — a dark, warm 'digital room' dashboard
problem: Most productivity tools feel like an admin dashboard — cold, demanding, and easy to abandon. I wanted a personal system that felt like coming home: somewhere to see the day's focus, protect what matters first, and keep small promises without guilt.
context: Studio OS is a personal life-operating-system I designed and built for myself, then redesigned around a 'digital room' metaphor — zones for today's focus, study, building, a calm corner, and a long-term horizon. It is fully offline: all state lives in localStorage, a calm ambient track plays through a simple HTML5 audio layer, and there is no backend or account. The public demo runs on a neutral demo persona so the concept can be explored without any of my private data.
constraints: Had to work fully offline with no backend, account, or API keys
Should feel warm and personal, not like a SaaS admin panel
Must stay genuinely useful day-to-day, not just look nice
The public demo must contain none of my real personal data
myRole: I designed the concept, the visual language, and built the whole thing — the room layout, the time-aware sessions, the local-first state engine, and a build-flagged demo-content layer that keeps my real data private.
solution: A single-page 'digital room' built on local-first state with defensive localStorage normalisers and SSR guards. The UI is time-aware (it suggests the right zone for the current hour), respects reduced-motion, and plays a calm ambient focus track through an HTML5 audio element (one bundled file plus optional user-added links). A demo build flag swaps in a neutral demo persona so the deployed version exposes the design without exposing me.
callout: The public demo is privacy-safe by construction: a build flag tree-shakes my real content out entirely and swaps in a neutral demo persona, while my private version stays local-only.
tradeoffs: Chose local-first (localStorage) over a backend — instant, private, and offline, at the cost of cross-device sync.
Kept the audio layer simple — one bundled ambient track through an HTML5 audio element, plus optional user-added links — instead of a heavier synthesis engine.
Built a demo-content layer gated by a build flag rather than maintaining two codebases.
whatIBuilt: A 'digital room' dashboard with distinct zones for focus, study, building, reset, and a long-term horizon.
A time-aware home that suggests the right activity for the current part of the day.
A local-first state layer with defensive JSON normalisers and safe parsing so bad data never crashes the app.
A calm ambient-audio layer: a bundled focus track played through an HTML5 audio element, plus a defensively-normalised list of user-added music links.
A build-flagged demo-content layer plus a 'reset to demo data' control for visitors.
whatILearned: How to build a genuinely offline, local-first app with resilient state handling.
Using a build flag and tree-shaking to ship a privacy-safe public build from a private codebase.
Wiring a simple, resilient audio layer with an HTML5 audio element and safe handling of user-supplied media links.
Designing for warmth and emotion in a tool — accessibility, reduced-motion, and tone all matter.
That the hardest part of a personal tool is making it calm enough that you actually keep using it.
results: Fully offline and credential-free; lint, strict type-check, and production build all pass clean.
The deployed demo build contains zero private personal data (verified by build-output scan).
Every route renders fast with a small client bundle.
nextBackendStep: Not applicable as a gap — the no-backend design is intentional. A natural future direction would be optional end-to-end-encrypted sync behind a hosted API if cross-device support is ever needed — also where I could apply the backend and cloud hosting I am actively learning.

screenshots.caption: The home 'room' — warm dark theme, zones, and the day's focus.
The daily focus view on a phone — small, honest proof for the day.
A calm reset corner — a short sequence to refocus when the day gets messy.
The long-term horizon — the 'why' behind the daily work.

repoNote: Validated with ESLint, strict TypeScript, and two clean builds (normal + demo); the demo build output was scanned to confirm zero private data ships.
